act-andrew-barr-kurrajong.summary: In the ACT Legislative Assembly debate on the Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022 (23 March 2023), Barr spoke in support of the bill, which narrowed exceptions for religious bodies so that, when providing public goods, services or employment, they may discriminate only on the ground of a person's religious conviction and not on other protected attributes such as sexuality, gender identity or marital status, while leaving greater freedom for religious observances such as the training and ordination of clergy. This is a stated position and a recorded contribution to debate.

sources.title: Week 2 - Thursday, 23 March 2023 - 10th Assembly Hansard

act-andrew-braddock-yerrabi.summary: In the Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022 debate (23 March 2023), Braddock (Greens) spoke in support of the bill, applauding the refinement of the Act's exceptions (including those affecting religious bodies) so that discrimination must be reasonable, proportionate and justifiable, and stated the Greens would oppose the Opposition's amendment that he said would effectively restore a blanket exemption for clubs to discriminate. This is a stated position and a recorded contribution to debate.

sources.title: Week 2 - Thursday, 23 March 2023 - 10th Assembly Hansard

act-ed-cocks-murrumbidgee.summary: In a Canberra Daily profile, Cocks listed 'freedom of worship' among the Liberal values he personally identifies with, naming it alongside freedom of speech, freedom of thought, freedom to own property and freedom to make choices in the market. This is a stated position.

sources.title: From public service to politics: Ed Cocks MLA

act-elizabeth-lee-kurrajong.summary: In the Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022 debate (23 March 2023), amendments circulated in Elizabeth Lee's name (moved on her behalf as she was absent) proposed inserting an objects clause stating the indivisibility and universality of human rights and that every person is free and equal in dignity, an extended commencement period, and a club/voluntary-body exception; the objects-clause amendment was put to a division and negatived 7-14. These are recorded proposed amendments attributed to her.

sources.title: Week 2 - Thursday, 23 March 2023 - 10th Assembly Hansard

act-jeremy-hanson-murrumbidgee.summary: In the Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022 debate (23 March 2023), Hanson said the Canberra Liberals would support the bill, stated that people should be protected from discrimination on the basis of their faith as well as other attributes, noted faith groups remained 'nervous' about possible unintended consequences, and moved amendments (including on behalf of Elizabeth Lee) seeking a longer commencement period and an objects clause asserting that all human rights are held with equal importance. This reflects stated positions and recorded contributions to debate.

sources.title: Week 2 - Thursday, 23 March 2023 - 10th Assembly Hansard

act-tara-cheyne-ginninderra.summary: As Minister for Human Rights, Cheyne introduced and championed the Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022; in the debate (23 March 2023) she stated the bill amended the exceptions for religious bodies to 'strike a better balance between the right to equality and the right to freedom of thought, conscience, religion and belief,' citing examples such as prayer groups still being able to exclude people who do not share the relevant religious conviction. This is a stated position and a recorded contribution to debate.

sources.title: Week 2 - Thursday, 23 March 2023 - 10th Assembly Hansard

nt-bill-yan-namatjira.summary: Recorded vote: in November 2022 (14th Assembly) Yan (listed as William Yan) voted against Labor's Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill, which removed exemptions that had allowed faith-based schools to hire staff sharing the school's religion.

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022

nt-chansey-paech-gwoja.summary: Recorded vote / sponsor: as Labor Attorney-General Paech introduced and voted for the 2022 Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill that removed religious-school hiring exemptions, describing criticism of its hate-speech provisions as 'fearmongering.' Stated position: in 2025 he opposed the CLP's amendments, posting that 'we do not support the CLP's attempt to water down our anti-discrimination laws.'

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022
Removal of anti-vilification protection unacceptable, NT Labor says
'Shameful': union slam NT's watering down of anti-discrimination laws

nt-dheran-young-daly.summary: Recorded vote: in November 2022 (14th Assembly) Young voted in favour of Labor's Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill, which removed exemptions that had allowed faith-based schools to hire staff sharing the school's religion and broadened vilification protections.

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022

nt-joshua-burgoyne-braitling.summary: Recorded vote: in November 2022 (14th Assembly) Burgoyne voted against Labor's Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill, which removed exemptions that had allowed faith-based schools to hire staff sharing the school's religion.

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022

nt-lia-finocchiaro-spillett.summary: Recorded vote: in November 2022 (14th Assembly) Finocchiaro voted against Labor's Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill. Stated position: she said the 2022 changes set an 'extremely low bar' on hate speech and that her government's 2025 amendments would 'remove the ridiculous inclusion of offending someone as some type of discrimination,' restoring protections for religious schools.

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022
Removal of anti-vilification protection unacceptable, NT Labor says

nt-marie-clare-boothby-brennan.summary: Recorded vote: in November 2022 (14th Assembly) Boothby voted against Labor's Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill, which removed exemptions allowing religious schools to hire on the basis of faith. Stated position: as Attorney-General she introduced the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2025 reinstating an exception for religious schools to hire on the basis of faith and narrowing vilification provisions, saying the changes aimed to restore 'freedom of speech, especially for individuals and religious communities ... without allowing [or] condoning hate speech.'

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022
CLP Restores Balance To Anti-Discrimination Laws
Removal of anti-vilification protection unacceptable, NT Labor says

nt-robyn-lambley-araluen.summary: Recorded vote: in November 2022 (14th Assembly) Lambley, as an independent, voted against Labor's Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill that removed religious-school hiring exemptions. Stated position: in the Australian Christian Lobby's 2024 NT election questionnaire she answered affirmatively to supporting repeal of the 2022 changes to protect faith-based schools, repeal of offence-based provisions, and completely protecting speech from the pulpit.

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022
NT Votes 2024

nt-selena-uibo-arnhem.summary: Recorded vote: in November 2022 (14th Assembly) Uibo voted in favour of Labor's Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill. Stated position: as Labor leader she said in 2025 that the CLP government's plan to remove vilification protections is 'unacceptable' and out of step with other Australian jurisdictions and international human rights conventions.

sources.title: How NT MPs voted on the Anti-Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022
Removal of anti-vilification protection unacceptable, NT Labor says
